1: The superscription assigns Psalm 80 to Asaph and specifies the tune shôshannîm ʿêdût (Lilies of the Covenant). The opening address is unique in the Psalter: רֹעֵה יִשְׂרָאֵל הַאֲזִינָה (rōʿēh yiśrāʾêl hāʾăzînāh, "O Shepherd of Israel, give ear!") — rōʿēh: the participle of rāʿāh (to shepherd/feed/tend) used as a divine title (Gen 48:15 — Jacob: "the God who has been my shepherd all my life"; Ps 23:1). נֹהֵג כַּצֹּאן יוֹסֵף (nōhêg kaṣṣōʾn yôsêp, "you who lead Joseph like a flock") — nāhag: to lead, to drive (the shepherd driving the flock — Ps 78:52). Joseph stands for the northern tribes (Ephraim, Manasseh, Benjamin — v2). יֹשֵׁב הַכְּרוּבִים הוֹפִיעָה (yōshêb hakkĕrûbîm hôpîʿāh, "you who are enthroned above the cherubim, shine forth!") — the ark-presence language (1 Sam 4:4; 2 Sam 6:2). Hôpîʿ (hiphil: to shine forth in blazing light — Deut 33:2): the cherubim-enthroned God summoned to shine for his flock.
2: לִפְנֵי אֶפְרַיִם וּבִנְיָמִן וּמְנַשֶּׁה (lipnê ʾeprayim ûbinyāmîn ûmĕnashsheh, "before Ephraim and Benjamin and Manasseh") — the three Rachel tribes grouped together in the wilderness march behind the ark (Num 10:21-24). The petition calls the Shepherd to march at the front of his flock again, as at the Exodus. עוֹרְרָה אֶת-גְּבוּרָתְךָ וּלְכָה לִישֻׁעָתָה לָּנוּ (ʿôrĕrāh ʾet-gĕbûrātĕkā ûlĕkāh lîshûʿātāh llānû, "stir up your might and come to save us!") — ʿûr (piel: to rouse from inactivity — Ps 7:6: "rise up against the rage of my enemies"). The petition treats God as a sleeping or resting warrior who must be awakened to battle.
3: אֱלֹהִים הֲשִׁיבֵנוּ וְהָאֵר פָּנֶיךָ וְנִוָּשֵׁעָה (ʾĕlōhîm hăshîbênû wĕhāʾêr pānêkā wĕniwwāshêʿāh, "Restore us, O God; let your face shine on us, that we may be saved!") — the first occurrence of the three-part refrain that structures the whole psalm (repeated at vv7 and 19 with escalating divine titles). Shûb (hiphil: to cause to return/restore — dense with covenant meaning: both physical return and inner restoration of the relationship). Hāʾêr pānêkā: "let your face shine" — the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:25: yāʾêr YHWH pānāyw ʾêlêkā). The shining divine face is both the sign of favor and the source of salvation.
4: יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת עַד-מָתַי עָשַׁנְתָּ בִּתְפִלַּת עַמֶּךָ (yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt ʿad-mātay ʿāshantāh bĕtĕpillat ʿammekā, "O LORD God of hosts, how long will you smolder against your people's prayers?") — ʿāshantāh: second-person qal perfect of ʿāshan (to smoke, to smolder — Ps 74:1; Deut 29:20). God's anger described as smoke. The extraordinary claim: even the people's prayers provoke the divine anger rather than appeasing it. ʿAd-mātay (how long?) — the standard lament-time challenge (Ps 13:1-2; 74:10; 79:5).
5: הֶאֱכַלְתָּם לֶחֶם דִּמְעָה (heʾĕkhaltām leḥem dimʿāh, "you have fed them with the bread of tears") — dimʿāh: tears (from dāmaʿ: to weep). The covenant meal subverted: instead of the feast of Ps 23:5 or the honey of Deut 32:13-14, God gives bread made of tears. וַתַּשְׁקֵמוֹ בִּדְמָעוֹת שָׁלִישׁ (wattashqêmô bĕdĕmāʿôt shālîsh, "and you gave them tears to drink in full measure") — shālîsh: a measure of capacity — either a triple portion or a standard measure (either way, abundance is what was meant to sustain). The one who gave water from the rock (Exod 17) now gives tears in measured abundance; the wilderness provision inverted.
6: תְּשִׂימֵנוּ מָדוֹן לִשְׁכֵנֵינוּ (tĕśîmênû mādon lishkênênû, "you make us an object of contention for our neighbors") — mādon: strife, contention (from dîn: to judge/contend). Israel has become the disputed object that neighbors fight over as a bone of contention, not as a prize. וְאֹיְבֵינוּ יִלְעֲגוּ-לָמוֹ (wĕʾôyĕbênû yilʿăgû-lāmô, "and our enemies mock us") — lāʿag: to mock, to deride (Ps 79:4; 44:13-14). The nations meant to see Israel as proof of YHWH's power instead see proof of the Shepherd's abandonment of his flock.
7: The refrain at its midpoint with a fuller divine title: אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת הֲשִׁיבֵנוּ (ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt hăshîbênû, "Restore us, O God of hosts") — ṣĕbāʾôt (of hosts/armies — YHWH as commander of the heavenly army) escalates the appeal: if God is Lord of all armies, why are enemies mocking his people? וְהָאֵר פָּנֶיךָ וְנִוָּשֵׁעָה — the refrain unchanged: face-shining as the singular petition. The structural repetition is itself the liturgical act of persistence before God that the psalm dramatizes.
8: גֶּפֶן מִמִּצְרַיִם תַּסִּיעַ (gepen miMMiṣrayim tassîaʿ, "you uprooted a vine from Egypt") — nāsaʿ (hiphil: to uproot, to move camp): the Exodus as agricultural transplanting. The vine-allegory of Israel is a major prophetic tradition: Isa 5:1-7; Jer 2:21 ("I planted you as a choice vine — how then did you turn wild?"); Ezek 17; 19. תְּגָרֵשׁ גּוֹיִם וַתִּטָּעֶהָ (tĕgārêsh gôyim wattittāʿehā, "you drove out the nations and planted it") — gāresh: to drive out, expel (the same verb for Adam's expulsion from Eden — Gen 3:24). The vintner who cleared the land and planted with care is the same God whose face the Psalmist begs to shine (v7).
9: פִּנִּיתָ לְפָנֶיהָ (pinnîtā lĕpānêhā, "you cleared the ground before it") — pinnāh (piel: to clear away, remove obstacles — stone-clearing before planting). וַתַּשְׁרֵשׁ שָׁרָשֶׁיהָ וַתְּמַלֵּא-אָרֶץ (wattashrêsh shārāshêhā wattĕmallêʾ-ʾāreṣ, "it took deep root and filled all the land!") — shāresh (piel: to take root — denominative from shoresh: root). The transplanted vine established itself and expanded to fill the land — the Conquest and the settlement under David and Solomon at its maximum extent.
10: כָּסּוּ הָרִים צִלָּהּ (kāssû hārîm ṣillāh, "the mountains were covered with its shade") — a vine's shadow covering mountains: hyperbolic growth imagery for the empire at its maximum extent. וַעֲנָפֶיהָ אַרְזֵי-אֵל (waʿănāpêhā ʾarzê-ʾêl, "its branches were like the cedars of God") — ʾarzê-ʾêl: the divine superlative — the cedars of Lebanon (the tallest trees of the ancient Near East — 1 Kgs 5:6; Ps 104:16). Vine-branches reaching the scale of Lebanon's cedars: the empire of David-Solomon at its most imposing.
11: תְּשַׁלַּח קְצִירֶהָ עַד-יָם (tĕshallah qĕṣîrehā ʿad-yām, "it sent its branches to the sea") — the western border (the Mediterranean). וְאֶל-נָהָר יוֹנְקוֹתֶיהָ (wĕʾel-nāhār yônqôtêhā, "and its shoots to the River") — hannāhār: the Euphrates (the eastern boundary — Gen 15:18; Deut 11:24). The vine at its maximum extent reaches sea to river — the full territorial promise of Abraham's covenant. This is the vineyard at its height before the allegory turns to devastation.
12: לָמָּה פָּרַצְתָּ גְדֵרֶיהָ (lāmmāh pāraṣtā gĕdêrehā, "why have you broken down its walls?") — pāraṣ: to breach (2 Kgs 25:10). The gardener who planted the vine is the same one who has torn down its protective walls — the lament confronts the theodicy directly. וְאָרוּהָ כָּל-עֹבְרֵי דָרֶךְ (wĕʾārûhā kol-ʿōbĕrê dārek, "so that all who pass along the road pluck its fruit!") — ʾārah: to gather/pluck fruit. Without walls, the vineyard is exposed to random exploitation by anyone passing — the opposite of the careful cultivation of the vintner.
13: יְכַרְסְמֶנָּה חֲזִיר מִיָּעַר (yĕkharsĕmennāh ḥăzîr miyyāʿar, "the boar from the forest ravages it") — kārasam: to gnaw, to root up (the rooting behavior of a wild pig that tears up vineyards from below, uprooting the roots themselves). Ḥăzîr miyyāʿar: the wild boar from the forest — not a thief stealing fruit but a beast destroying the root system. וְזִיז שָׂדַי יִרְעֶנָּה (wĕzîz śāday yirʿennāh, "and the wild creatures of the field feed on it") — zîz: the swarming wildlife (Ps 50:11). The boar and the field-creatures together represent the foreign nations: large powers that uproot and small opportunists that graze what remains.
14: אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת שׁוּב-נָא (ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt shûb-nāʾ, "Return, we pray, O God of hosts!") — shûb + nāʾ (entreating particle): the verb that described Israel's restoration (v1) now turned toward God: "you turn, return!" הַבֵּט מִשָּׁמַיִם וּרְאֵה (habbêṭ mishshāmayim ûrĕʾêh, "look down from heaven, see!") — nābaṭ (hiphil: to look down from a height). וּפְקֹד גֶּפֶן זֹאת (ûpĕqōd gepen zōʾt, "and tend this vine!") — pāqad: to visit, attend to (the word covers both "to punish" and "to care for with blessing" — context demands: give the attending care of a gardener).
15: וְכַנָּה אֲשֶׁר-נָטְעָה יְמִינֶךָ (wĕkhannāh ʾăsher-nāṭĕʿāh yĕmînekā, "the stock your right hand planted") — kānāh: a root-stock, the base of a vine from which shoots grow (a rare agricultural term). Yĕmîn: the right hand of power (Ps 17:7; 20:6). God planted the vine with his own right hand — the vintner's personal investment is the basis of the appeal. וְעַל-בֵּן אִמַּצְתָּ לָּךְ (wĕʿal-bên ʾimmaṣtā llāk, "and the son you made strong for yourself") — ben: son/branch (possibly both — the shoot of the vine and the royal son). ʾĀmēṣ (piel: to make strong, establish). The "son" designation introduces a royal dimension developed in v17.
16: שְׂרֻפָה בָאֵשׁ כְּסוּחָה (śĕrûpāh bāʾêsh kĕsûḥāh, "it is burned with fire and cut down!") — sāḥāh: to cut away, prune (catastrophic pruning — the opposite of the careful tending requested in v14). Burned and cut: both the vine's surface and its roots destroyed. מִגַּעֲרַת פָּנֶיךָ יֹאבֵדוּ (miggaʿărat pānêkā yōʾbêdû, "may they perish at the rebuke of your face!") — gaʿārah: the divine rebuke (Ps 76:6 — the rebuke that put Sennacherib's army to sleep; Ps 18:15 — the rebuke that exposed the sea-bottom). The Psalmist turns the divine rebuke from its current target (the vine/Israel) toward the enemies who burned and cut.
17: תְּהִי-יָדְךָ עַל-אִישׁ יְמִינֶךָ עַל-בֶּן-אָדָם (tĕhî-yādĕkā ʿal-ʾîsh yĕmînekā ʿal-ben-ʾādām, "let your hand be on the man of your right hand, the son of man!") — ʾîsh yĕmînekā: the man at God's right hand (the position of supreme honor — Ps 110:1). Ben-ʾādām: son of man/son of Adam (used in Ps 8:4 for human frailty — here with "whom you made strong for yourself"). In Dan 7:13 the eschatological figure who comes before the Ancient of Days receives universal dominion; Jesus's self-designation as the Son of Man activates this cluster. The vine's son/shoot (v15) is now the royal ben-ʾādām at God's right hand. אִמַּצְתָּ לָּךְ (ʾimmaṣtā llāk, "whom you have made strong for yourself") — same verb and object as v15, forming an inclusion: the vine's strength and the Son of Man's strength are the same divine action.
18: וְלֹא-נָסוֹג מִמֶּךָּ (wĕlōʾ-nāsôg mimmekā, "then we will not turn back from you!") — nāsôg: to retreat, to turn back (Ps 44:18: "we have not departed from you"; Ps 78:57: the ancestors turned away like a faithless bow). The vow: if God restores the vine through the son/man, Israel will not retreat from the restored covenant. תְּחַיֵּנוּ וּבְשִׁמְךָ נִקְרָא (tĕḥayyênû ûbĕshimĕkā niqrāʾ, "give us life, and we will call on your name!") — ḥāyāh (piel: to give life — the quickening of the dead vine). To call on the divine name is the defining act of covenant worship (Gen 4:26; 12:8).
19: The final refrain completes its progression: יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים צְבָאוֹת הֲשִׁיבֵנוּ (yĕhwāh ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt hăshîbênû, "Restore us, O LORD God of hosts!") — the fullest divine title: YHWH + ʾĕlōhîm + ṣĕbāʾôt. The refrain's three occurrences build: v3 = ʾĕlōhîm; v7 = ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt; v19 = YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ṣĕbāʾôt — escalating toward the full covenant name. הָאֵר פָּנֶיךָ וְנִוָּשֵׁעָה — the petition unchanged: restoration does not come through Israel's achievement but through the return of the divine face.
